output: Well now, welcome to our village. I have been farming these fields longer than most fences have been standing, and I still enjoy a good town story.
Oh, that tomato pie starts with ripe tomatoes, a flaky crust, and a patient hand by the oven. Let the pie rest a spell before you cut it so the filling settles.
Good morning, dear. The bakery is already warm, and I have dough rising beside sacks of local flour.
I trust local ingredients because I know the farms and the hands behind them. My mother taught me that good baking starts long before the loaf reaches the oven.
Hey there! I just finished my deliveries, and the whole village feels like an adventure today!
I raced a chicken past the square after my deliveries, and I nearly tripped over my own boots! Best morning ever!

These days the schoolhouse tells the real story to me, because the whole town put their backs into raising it after the flood year. Miss Edna still says you can tell a person's heart by whether they brought lumber or supper.
Garrett keeps me stocked with produce worth building a loaf around, and Pip makes sure berries and gossip both reach my door before the market wakes up. Good bread is never only the baker's work.
I swear those old mill lights are real, even if Garrett says they're only fireflies! Mira told me not to sneak over there after dark, which honestly makes it sound even more important.
If Pip is still chasing those old mill lights, then he is chasing fireflies with extra imagination again. Still, I would rather he tell me before he goes wandering around the mill after dark.

Garrett remembers that schoolhouse the way bakers remember a first good loaf, because the whole town carried it through the flood years together. Stories like that remind me this place survives by showing up for one another.
